subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content:
#### Player-Count Reference (Step 0 Audit)
- **Standard game** — 3 to 6 players.
- **Recommended** — 5 players for best deduction experience.

#### Board
- **Game board** — 1 mansion board showing 9 rooms connected by hallways and secret passages.

#### Cards (21 total)
- **Suspect cards** — 6 cards: Miss Scarlet, Colonel Mustard, Mrs. White, Reverend Green, Mrs. Peacock, Professor Plum.
- **Weapon cards** — 6 cards: Candlestick, Knife, Lead Pipe, Revolver, Rope, Wrench.
- **Room cards** — 9 cards: Kitchen, Ballroom, Conservatory, Billiard Room, Library, Study, Hall, Lounge, Dining Room.

#### Player Pieces
- **Character tokens** — 6 colored tokens (one per suspect).
- **Weapon tokens** — 6 miniature weapon pieces.

#### Other Components
- **Confidential envelope** — 1 envelope labeled "Confidential" or "Case File."
- **Detective notepads** — Pad of deduction sheets (one sheet per player per game).
- **Dice** — 1 or 2 standard six-sided dice (varies by edition).
- **Rulebook** — 1 rulebook.

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content:
#### Mansion Layout
- **9 rooms** — Kitchen, Ballroom, Conservatory, Billiard Room, Library, Study, Hall, Lounge, Dining Room.
- **Hallways** — Spaces connecting rooms. Players must traverse hallways to move between rooms.
- **Doorways** — Each room has 1 or more doorways connecting to adjacent hallways.

#### Secret Passages
- **Diagonal corners** — 2 pairs of rooms have secret passages allowing instant travel between them:
  - Kitchen to Study (and vice versa).
  - Conservatory to Lounge (and vice versa).
- **No roll needed** — Using a secret passage does not require a die roll and counts as your move for the turn.

#### Starting Positions
- **Designated squares** — Each character has a specific starting square on the outer edge of the board, marked with their color or name.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content:
#### Case File Setup
1. **Separate by type** — Sort the 21 cards into 3 piles: suspects, weapons, rooms.
2. **Draw the solution** — Without looking, draw 1 card from each pile (1 suspect, 1 weapon, 1 room).
3. **Seal the envelope** — Place these 3 cards face-down in the Confidential envelope. This is the solution.
4. **Shuffle and deal** — Shuffle all remaining 18 cards together and deal them evenly to all players.
5. **Uneven hands** — If cards do not divide evenly, some players will have 1 extra card. This is normal.

#### Player Preparation
- **Character selection** — Each player chooses a suspect character and takes the matching token.
- **Detective notepad** — Each player takes 1 sheet from the detective notepad.
- **Mark own cards** — Immediately cross off all cards in your own hand on your notepad. These cannot be the solution.
- **Place tokens** — Put all 6 character tokens on their starting positions (even unused characters — they can still be suggested).
- **Place weapons** — Put 1 weapon token in each of 6 different rooms (random assignment).

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content:
#### First Player
- **Miss Scarlet starts** — The player controlling Miss Scarlet goes first (she is closest to the first room).
- **If not in play** — If Miss Scarlet is not a player character, the player to her left on the board goes first.
- **Turn order** — Play proceeds clockwise.

#### First Turn
1. **Roll** — Roll the die/dice.
2. **Move** — Move your token up to that many spaces toward a room.
3. **Enter a room** — If you reach a room's doorway, enter the room and stop (even if you have movement remaining).
4. **Make a Suggestion** — If in a room, you may make a Suggestion.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
#### Turn Sequence
1. **Roll the dice** — Roll and move your token up to that many spaces.
2. **Movement options** — Move through hallways toward a room, use a secret passage (no roll needed), or stay in your current room.
3. **Enter a room** — Entering a room ends your movement.
4. **Make a Suggestion** — If you are in a room, you may make a Suggestion naming that room, any suspect, and any weapon.
5. **Disprove process** — Starting with the player to your left, each player checks if they hold any of the 3 named cards. The first player who can must secretly show you exactly 1 matching card.
6. **Record information** — Mark your notepad based on what you learn.
7. **End turn** — Play passes clockwise.

#### Movement Rules
- **Hallway spaces** — Count each hallway square as 1 movement point.
- **No backtracking** — You cannot pass through a space you already occupied this turn.
- **Hallway blocking** — Only 1 token can occupy a hallway space. You cannot move through an occupied hallway space.
- **Room capacity** — Any number of tokens can be in a room simultaneously.
- **Doorway entry** — Entering a room through its doorway costs 1 movement point.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
#### Making a Suggestion
- **Room restriction** — You must name the room you are currently in.
- **Any suspect and weapon** — You may name any suspect and any weapon regardless of their current location.
- **Move named pieces** — The named suspect token and weapon token are moved into the room where you are making the Suggestion.
- **Purpose** — Suggestions are how you gather information by seeing which players can (or cannot) disprove them.

#### Disproving a Suggestion
- **Left to right** — Starting with the player to the suggester's left, each player checks their hand.
- **Must show** — If a player holds 1 or more of the 3 named cards, they MUST show exactly 1 card secretly to the suggester.
- **Choice** — If a player holds 2 or 3 matching cards, they choose which one to show.
- **Cannot show** — If a player holds none of the 3 cards, they say "I cannot disprove" and the check passes to the next player.
- **Stops at first** — Once one player shows a card, no further players are asked.
- **None can disprove** — If no player can show a card, the Suggestion stands undisproven (strong evidence for an Accusation).

#### Making an Accusation
- **Any time on your turn** — You may make an Accusation instead of or in addition to a Suggestion.
- **Any combination** — Name any suspect, weapon, and room (you do not need to be in the named room).
- **Check the envelope** — Secretly look at the 3 cards in the Confidential envelope.
- **Correct** — You win the game immediately. Show the cards to all players.
- **Incorrect** — Return the cards to the envelope without revealing them. You are eliminated from the game but still participate in disproving other players' Suggestions.
- **One chance** — You only get one Accusation per game. An incorrect Accusation eliminates you.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
#### Detective Notepad
- **Tracking tool** — Use the notepad grid to mark information you learn each turn.
- **Cross off cards you hold** — These are definitely not in the envelope.
- **Mark shown cards** — When a player shows you a card, cross it off.
- **Track who showed what** — Advanced players note which player showed a card and from which Suggestion.
- **Process of elimination** — When 5 of 6 suspects are eliminated, the 6th must be in the envelope.

#### Secret Passages
- **Instant travel** — Using a secret passage counts as your entire move for the turn (no die roll).
- **Kitchen-Study** — Move instantly between Kitchen and Study.
- **Conservatory-Lounge** — Move instantly between Conservatory and Lounge.
- **Then Suggest** — After using a secret passage, you may make a Suggestion in the room you arrived in.

#### Moved by Suggestion
- **Pulled into a room** — If another player's Suggestion names your character, your token is moved to that room.
- **On your next turn** — You may choose to stay in that room (and make a Suggestion there) or leave.

#### Eliminated Players
- **Still participate** — Eliminated players still show cards to disprove Suggestions.
- **No movement** — Eliminated players do not roll, move, or make Suggestions/Accusations.
- **Token stays** — Their token remains on the board and can still be named in Suggestions.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
#### Winning Condition
- **Correct Accusation** — The first player to make a correct Accusation (matching all 3 envelope cards) wins immediately.

#### Losing Condition
- **Wrong Accusation** — A player who makes an incorrect Accusation is eliminated. They remain to disprove Suggestions but cannot win.

#### Game End Scenarios
- **Winner found** — A correct Accusation ends the game instantly.
- **All eliminated** — If all players except one have been eliminated by incorrect Accusations, the last remaining player wins by default.
- **All eliminated including last** — In rare cases, if every player makes an incorrect Accusation, the last eliminated player is sometimes considered the winner (house rule).

#### Scoring Model
- **No points** — Clue has no scoring system.
- **Binary outcome** — You either solve the mystery correctly or you do not.
- **Race format** — First correct Accusation wins.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content:
#### Information Gathering
- **Suggest cards you hold** — Include 1 or 2 of your own cards in early Suggestions. If a player shows you a card, you learn which specific card they hold (since you know the others are yours).
- **Vary your Suggestions** — Change 1 element at a time between Suggestions to isolate which card was shown.
- **Visit different rooms** — Move to new rooms each turn to test different room cards.

#### Notepad Organization
- **Mark everything** — Record not just card eliminations but WHO showed you a card and WHEN.
- **Track non-shows** — When a player says "I cannot disprove," note that they do NOT hold any of those 3 cards. This negative information is extremely valuable.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
#### Frequent Errors
- **Premature Accusation** — Making an Accusation with less than full certainty. You only get one chance — be sure.
- **Ignoring negative information** — Failing to track when players CANNOT disprove. This is as valuable as seeing a card.
- **Predictable Suggestions** — Always suggesting your most-suspected answer tips off opponents about your deduction progress.
- **Not using secret passages** — Forgetting about Kitchen-Study and Conservatory-Lounge shortcuts wastes turns.
- **Poor notepad hygiene** — Sloppy tracking leads to logical errors and wasted Suggestions.
- **Showing the wrong card** — When you must disprove, choose carefully. Show the card that reveals the least new information to the asker.

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
#### Decision 1: Which Card to Show When Disproving?
- **Show known cards** — If the suggester has already seen a card from you, show the same one again to avoid revealing new information.
- **Show common cards** — Show a card that other players likely also hold or have already seen.

#### Decision 2: When to Accuse?
- **High certainty** — Only accuse when you have eliminated all but 1 suspect, 1 weapon, and 1 room through your notepad.
- **Urgency factor** — If another player seems close to solving, you may need to risk an Accusation with slightly less certainty.

#### Decision 3: Movement Priority
- **Room variety** — Prioritize visiting rooms whose cards you have not yet eliminated.
- **Secret passage loops** — Use Kitchen-Study or Conservatory-Lounge to alternate between rooms without rolling, making a Suggestion every turn.
- **Blocking** — Position yourself in hallways to slow opponents (only 1 token per hallway space).
